text: [...] we have been expressly evolved by nature-with all our impulses and deepest instincts-for the purpose of solving the economic problem. If the economic problem is solved, mankind will be deprived of its traditional purpose. Will this be a benefit? If one believes at all in the real values of life, the prospect at least opens up the possibility of benefit. Yet I think with dread of the readjustment of the habits and instincts of the ordinary man, bred into him for countless generations, which he may be asked to discard within a few decades.
author: John Maynard Keynes

didItHoldUp.analysis: Keynes asked the deepest question in economics: what happens when we solve scarcity? His dread was prophetic. In wealthy nations, the economic problem is largely solved — most people have food, shelter, and material comfort their grandparents couldn't imagine. But the existential crisis Keynes feared has arrived differently than expected. Instead of leisure and philosophy, we got consumerism and anxiety. The 'readjustment' he worried about shows up as epidemic depression, addiction, and a crisis of meaning in the world's richest societies. We solved the economic problem. We didn't solve the human one.
